title: Rebates: Private health bosses call for gold cover overhaul
full_text:
Insurers and hospital operators warn that as many as 200,000 Australians will downgrade their top-tier health cover due to cost-of-living pressures and the Albanese government's decision to axe a tax rebate for elderly Australians, putting huge pressure on public hospitals.
Chief executives from across the private health sector on Tuesday called for the government to scrap the four-tiered gold, silver, bronze and basic product system introduced by the Morrison government seven years ago, saying it was out of date and confusing.
"I feel sorry for the consumer. You almost need a PhD to understand the products these days," Sue Williams, chief executive of hospital group Cabrini Health, told The Australian Financial Review Insurance Summit.
Health insurers also called for an overhaul of the product tiers following a mass exodus of members from the most expensive forms of cover, arguing it was too rigid. More than 14 million Australians have private health insurance.
Rachel David, CEO of industry group Private Healthcare Australia, said modelling showed 200,000 will downgrade their health insurance policies due to cost-of-living pressures. That comes after the government predicted that 44,000 would drop their private health insurance following Labor's changes to the rebate for people aged over 65, which were part of broader measures in the federal budget to redistribute wealth to younger Australians.
"The people most likely to drop out are most likely to be the sickest, and they will go on to public hospital waiting lists and take up beds in public hospitals. So we have a social license issue to make sure, particularly those that have paid health insurance for decades, that they're defended," she said.
"There's also going to be an impact on private hospitals as well, particularly those in rural and regional areas."
The Albanese government is considering changes to private health insurance product tiers as part of a broader overhaul aimed at making the hospital sector more viable.
Labor wants to avoid hospital closures because they put pressure on the public system, but is unwilling to bail out private operators.
Damien Bruce, the chief executive of Calvary Healthcare, which is part of a consortium looking to acquire the country's second-largest hospital group, Healthscope, said the sector faced challenges, but he believed an expanded hospital network linked to aged care would benefit the private health system.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Michael Smith
heading: Health and Disability Insurance
